An operations team must launch {num_tasks} independent work orders on one shared facility over a discrete calendar. The facility can provide at most {resource_capacity} units of capacity at any time tick, and each order continuously consumes a fixed amount of that capacity while it is being processed. Each order requires a fixed number of consecutive ticks; once it begins it must proceed without pausing. Every order becomes ready at its own ready time and must be launched no later than its last allowed start time. In addition, each order has a preferred (target) launch tick; starting later than that target creates a service delay. The delay for an order is the amount of time its chosen start tick exceeds its target (and is zero if it starts on or before the target). The plan must choose exactly one start tick for each order within its allowed window, while never exceeding shared capacity at any tick, and minimize the sum of delays across all orders. The calendar spans ticks {min_time} through {max_time}, covering {horizon} ticks in total.

Parameters (JSON): {"horizon": "201", "max_time": "200", "min_time": "0", "num_tasks": "50", "resource_capacity": "25"}
Associated data files:
- parameters.path: data/parameters.csv
- parameters.description: Global scenario settings. Each row defines a single scalar setting that applies to the whole instance, such as the number of jobs, the machine's total capacity, and the bounds of the discrete timeline used to evaluate capacity usage.
Data format:
| parameter | value |
|----------|-------|
| <string> | <string_or_number> |
- tasks.path: data/tasks.csv
- tasks.description: Job-level data. Each row corresponds to one job. The columns describe how long the job runs, how much shared facility capacity it consumes while running, when it becomes eligible to start, the last time it is still allowed to start, and the preferred (target) start tick used to measure service delay. The allowed start window is defined by `release_time` and `latest_start`; start ticks outside this window are not permitted.
Data format:
| task_id | duration | resource_demand | release_time | latest_start | target_start |
|--------|----------|-----------------|--------------|--------------|--------------|
| <int>  | <int>    | <int>           | <int>        | <int>        | <int>        |

The National Heritage Preservation Center manages a restoration initiative for {n} artifacts, which are categorized into {m} distinct historical collections. The mapping between artifacts and collections is provided in the file `project_teams` (note that a single artifact may be associated with multiple collections due to cross-cultural influences). The facility has installed {k} advanced Type-Z Preservation Chambers. Due to the delicate atmospheric controls required, each chamber has a strict physical limit of {c} artifacts. Artifacts that are not selected for these high-tech chambers will remain in the Standard Storage Depot. Artifacts with identification numbers divisible by 5 have been flagged for a specialized digital cataloging process scheduled for the next fiscal quarter. The operational cost for the Standard Storage Depot is calculated at 5 monetary units per item daily, while the Type-Z Chambers have a fixed daily operation fee. To maintain the 'Historical Resonance Integrity' necessary for accurate restoration, the center enforces a strict location protocol: For any specific historical collection, all items from that collection that are assigned to Type-Z Chambers must be housed within the SAME chamber. Placing items from one collection into a Type-Z Chamber and others from the same collection into the Standard Storage Depot is permitted, but scattering items of a single collection across multiple Type-Z Chambers is strictly prohibited to prevent cross-chamber calibration errors. Determine the maximum number of artifacts that can be successfully assigned to the Type-Z Preservation Chambers.

A secure research facility has {n} researchers belonging to {m} different project groups. The membership structure is detailed in the file `project_teams` (a researcher can belong to multiple groups). The facility contains {k} enclosed secure labs. Due to ventilation and security constraints, each lab can accommodate at most {c} researchers. To maximize security, the goal is to assign as many researchers as possible to these secure labs. Researchers who cannot be assigned to a lab must work in the 'Public Hall'. To ensure collaboration efficiency and prevent unauthorized cross-lab data flow, the following rule must be strictly followed: **For any given project group, all members assigned to secure labs must be placed in the SAME lab.** (i.e., members of the same group cannot be split across different secure labs; they must either be in the designated lab for that group or in the Public Hall). Find the maximum number of researchers that can be assigned to the secure labs under these constraints.

The Imperial Chronology Division manages a collection of {n} temporal artifacts recovered from {m} distinct timelines. The lineage of each artifact is documented in the file `project_teams` (note that a single artifact may possess quantum signatures linking it to multiple timelines). To prevent degradation, these artifacts can be stored in {k} Stasis Chambers. Due to the geometric constraints of the containment fields, each Stasis Chamber has a hard limit of holding at most {c} artifacts. Artifacts that are not assigned to a Stasis Chamber must remain in the 'Entropy Buffer' zone. To maintain the stability of the space-time continuum, the 'Isotopic Coherence Protocol' must be strictly enforced: For any given timeline, all artifacts from that timeline that are secured within the Stasis Chambers must be located in the exact same chamber. It is strictly forbidden to fragment a timeline's preserved artifacts across two or more different Stasis Chambers, as this causes a causality loop; however, artifacts remaining in the Entropy Buffer are considered inert and do not affect this coherence constraint. Additionally, artifacts with odd-numbered IDs emit Alpha radiation while even-numbered ones emit Beta radiation; Alpha emitters require the chamber's initialization sequence to run for 15 minutes longer than standard, but all chambers are equipped to handle both radiation types without capacity reduction. The facility operates on a strict energy budget where each active chamber consumes 450 Gigajoules per cycle. The primary objective is to determine the maximum total quantity of artifacts that can be successfully secured within the Stasis Chambers while adhering to the capacity limits and the Isotopic Coherence Protocol.

The 'Horizon-IX' deep-space outpost is currently managed by {n} specialists who are organized into {m} distinct functional mission clusters. The specific roster showing which specialist belongs to which cluster is provided in the file `project_teams` (note that cross-trained specialists may belong to multiple clusters). The outpost is equipped with {k} High-Security Habitation Modules (HSHMs). Due to the limitations of the CO2 scrubbing systems and emergency escape pod capacity, each HSHM can safely sustain a maximum of {c} specialists. The HSHMs are arranged in a ring structure surrounding the central core, with a travel time of approximately 45 seconds between adjacent modules, a factor often considered for emergency evacuation protocols. To optimize the station's operational security, the administration aims to accommodate the maximum possible number of specialists within these secure HSHMs. Specialists who cannot be accommodated in an HSHM must reside in the 'General Assembly Area', where water recycling efficiency is 15% lower than in the secure modules, leading to higher resource consumption per capita. A critical bio-security regulation known as the 'Unit Cohesion Protocol' dictates the assignment logic: for any given mission cluster, the subset of its members selected for HSHM residency must be consolidated within a single, identical HSHM to maintain quarantine integrity. It is strictly prohibited to fragment the HSHM-assigned members of a single cluster across two or more different HSHMs. Members of a cluster can be split between their designated HSHM and the General Assembly Area. Determine the maximum number of specialists that can be assigned to the HSHMs subject to these capacity and cohesion constraints.

A high-performance computing (HPC) cluster manager is scheduling {n} computational threads which support {m} distinct microservices. The dependency mapping is provided in `project_teams` (note that due to shared libraries, a single thread may support multiple microservices). The cluster features {k} Tensor Processing Units (TPUs). Each TPU has a hard hardware concurrency limit of {c} threads. TPUs with prime number identifiers are connected via a low-latency InfiniBand fabric, offering 15% faster interconnect speeds compared to the standard Ethernet backplane used by composite-numbered TPUs. The system's power management protocol dictates that every active thread consumes 12W of power, while idle slots consume 2W. The primary objective is to offload the maximum number of threads onto these TPUs. To maintain memory coherence and minimize cross-chip latency, the scheduler enforces a strict locality rule: for any given microservice, all of its threads that are offloaded to TPUs must be executed within the same physical TPU node. It is strictly forbidden to distribute threads from a single microservice across two or more different TPUs. Threads that cannot be accommodated on the TPUs will be executed on the legacy CPU standard pool. Determine the maximum total number of threads that can be assigned to the TPU nodes under these configuration rules.

We are overseeing the resource orchestration for a distributed high-performance computing (HPC) cluster tasked with processing {n_services} distinct cryptographic shard modules. These modules are to be distributed across a physical infrastructure consisting of {n_racks} isolation zones, indexed from 0 to {n_racks}-1. Zones with prime number indices are equipped with liquid cooling systems, while composite indexed zones utilize standard air cooling; however, the thermal management method does not alter the logical compatibility of the deployment. Each shard module depends on a specific subset of {n_policies} security protocol keys (IDs 0 to {n_policies}-1) to function. Deployment rules mandate that a module can only be instantiated in a zone where all its required protocol keys are loaded and active. To ensure data sovereignty and minimize attack surfaces, strict replication rules apply to the protocol keys: a key is considered secure if loaded in exactly one zone (0 risk score). If a key must be replicated to a second zone for reachability, it incurs a synchronization latency penalty of 1 point. Loading the same key into more than two zones triggers a consensus failure and is strictly prohibited. The system operates under a specific Service Level Agreement (SLA) that prioritizes high availability through redundancy. Consequently, the optimization goal is to maximize the 'Resilience Score', calculated as the count of actively utilized zones multiplied by a weighting factor of {min_policies_per_rack}, minus the total synchronization latency penalties accumulated from replicated keys. Furthermore, to prevent resource fragmentation, the deployment must maintain a minimum key density: the average number of active protocol keys per utilized zone must be no less than {min_policies_per_rack}. Note that the total power consumption of the cluster is capped at 450kW, and zones 0 through 4 are designated for priority maintenance during the first quarter, though they remain fully operational for this allocation task.

| 0 | 97 |
| 1 | 97 |
| ... | ... |

We are overseeing the orchestration of a high-security distributed computing grid consisting of {n_services} distinct computational modules and {n_racks} physical server clusters (indexed 0 to {n_racks}-1). The system relies on {n_policies} proprietary cryptographic keys (policies), indexed accordingly. Each computational module has a unique dependency profile requiring a specific subset of these keys to function; some lightweight modules may run without keys. The modules vary in memory footprint, ranging from 2GB to 64GB, though containerization standardizes their deployment slots. Deployment is governed by strict vendor licensing and physical constraints: 1. A module can only be initialized on a server cluster that has all its required keys actively loaded in memory. 2. Key licensing follows a tiered structure: loading a key on a single cluster is covered by the base subscription (0 penalty); mirroring the key to a second cluster for redundancy incurs a surcharge of 1 point; replicating a key to three or more clusters triggers a license violation and is strictly forbidden. Server clusters 0 through 4 are designated as 'Legacy' hardware while the remainder are 'Next-Gen', yet they process keys identically. The optimization goal is to maximize the grid's utility score, calculated as the total number of active server clusters multiplied by a stability factor of {min_policies_per_rack}, minus the total licensing surcharges (weighted at 1). Furthermore, to justify the energy overhead of activating a cluster, the grid must maintain a resource density where the average number of keys loaded per active cluster is no less than {min_policies_per_rack}.

This instance describes dispatch planning for short-haul container moves around a terminal and its nearby transfer points. There are {N} transport orders and {M} available chassis. The planner must decide a one-to-one pairing so that every order is covered and every chassis is used exactly once. Pairing is allowed for any order-chassis combination; the data provide explicit cost entries for some pairings and all other pairings use an implicit baseline cost of {baseline_assignment_cost}. The plan also tracks {num_time_vars} event timestamps for key visits or handoffs. Operational policies are given as standardized rule records that link pairing choices to timing consistency requirements and to shared-facility limits aggregated over a single referenced order set (the set may cover only a subset of orders). In this instance, each order has a designated primary chassis option that shares the same identifier as the order, and timing-shift rules describe how the required time gap changes when switching from that primary option to an alternative. Each rule table uses a uniform comparison direction code, given by {pair_time_link_default_sense} for timing-shift rules and {chassis_time_default_sense} for group-aggregation rules. Timing-shift rules also rely on a shared large adjustment constant {pair_time_link_big_m}. All identifiers in the data files are zero-based. The goal is to find a feasible plan with minimum total cost.

Associated data files:

- assignment_costs.description: Cost entries associated with pairing an order and a chassis. Pairing is allowed for any order-chassis combination; this file only lists explicit cost entries. Any pairing not listed should be treated as having the baseline (implicit) cost contribution. Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | order_id | chassis_id | cost |
| <int> | <int> | <float> |
- chassis_time_rules.path: data/chassis_time_rules.csv
- chassis_time_rules.description: Aggregate rule records that connect how a specific chassis is used across the referenced order set in order_groups.csv with one event timestamp. The comparison direction and the threshold are uniform across all rows and are provided in problem_structure.csv. Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | chassis_id | time_id | assignment_coefficient |
| <int> | <int> | <float> |
- order_groups.path: data/order_groups.csv
- order_groups.description: Orders referenced by the aggregate rules in chassis_time_rules.csv. Each row lists one order identifier that belongs to the single aggregate set used by this instance (the set may cover only a subset of all orders). Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | order_id |
| <int> |
- pair_time_link_rules.path: data/pair_time_link_rules.csv
- pair_time_link_rules.description: Rule records that connect an order's chassis choice to the allowed time gap between two event timestamps. Each row references one order, one alternative chassis option for that order, and two timestamps. The designated primary chassis for an order is the chassis with the same identifier as the order. The comparison direction and the large adjustment constant are uniform across all rows and are provided in problem_structure.csv. Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | rhs | order_id | chassis_alternative | time_from | time_to | fixed_choice_coefficient |
| <float> | <int> | <int> | <int> | <int> | <float> |
- problem_structure.path: data/problem_structure.csv
- problem_structure.description: Instance-level scalar metadata including dimensions and uniform default constants used by the rule tables. Data format: | parameter | value |
| <string> | <string> |
- time_bounds.path: data/time_bounds.csv
- time_bounds.description: Allowed ranges for event timestamps. Each row provides a lower and upper limit for one timestamp identifier. Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | time_id | lower_bound | upper_bound |
| <int> | <float> | <float> |

The affected set of orders is provided by `order_groups.csv`. The comparison direction and the right-hand-side threshold are uniform across all rows and are provided in `problem_structure.csv`. The implemented rule template is: time(time_id) + assignment_coefficient * sum_{order in order_groups.csv} assign(order, chassis_id) (comparison_direction) (threshold). Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | chassis_id | time_id | assignment_coefficient |\n| <int> | <int> | <float> |
- order_groups.path: data/order_groups.csv
- order_groups.description: Orders referenced by the aggregate rules in `chassis_time_rules.csv`. Each row lists one order identifier that belongs to the single aggregate set used by this instance (the set may cover only a subset of all orders). Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | order_id |\n| <int> |
- pair_time_link_rules.path: data/pair_time_link_rules.csv
- pair_time_link_rules.description: Rule records that connect an order's chassis choice to the allowed time gap between two event timestamps. Each row references one order, one alternative chassis option for that order, and two timestamps, and it encodes a single linear rule template that shifts the required time gap depending on whether the order keeps its designated primary chassis or switches to the alternative. The designated primary chassis for an order is the chassis with the same identifier as the order. The rule uses the one-to-one assignment indicators for (order_id, primary_chassis) and (order_id, chassis_alternative). The comparison direction and the large adjustment constant are uniform across all rows and are provided in `problem_structure.csv`. The implemented rule template is: (time_to - time_from) + fixed_choice_coefficient * assign(order_id, primary_chassis) - (large_adjustment_constant) * assign(order_id, chassis_alternative) (comparison_direction) rhs. Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | rhs | order_id | chassis_alternative | time_from | time_to | fixed_choice_coefficient |\n| <float> | <int> | <int> | <int> | <int> | <float> |
- problem_structure.path: data/problem_structure.csv
- problem_structure.description: Instance-level scalar metadata for driving loops and sanity checks without hard-coding dimensions. This file provides the key instance dimensions referenced in the narrative (such as the number of orders, chassis, and timestamps) as well as the uniform default constants used by the rule tables (baseline cost, comparison direction codes, shared adjustment constant, and shared thresholds). Data format: | parameter | value |\n| <string> | <string> |
- time_bounds.path: data/time_bounds.csv
- time_bounds.description: Allowed ranges for event timestamps. Each row provides a lower and upper limit for one timestamp identifier. Identifiers are zero-based integers. Data format: | time_id | lower_bound | upper_bound |\n| <int> | <float> | <float> |

This instance describes dispatch planning for short-haul container moves around a terminal and its nearby transfer points. There are {N} transport orders and {M} available chassis. The planner must decide a one-to-one pairing so that every order is covered and every chassis is used exactly once. Pairing is allowed for any order-chassis combination; the data provide explicit cost entries for some pairings and all other pairings use an implicit baseline cost of {baseline_assignment_cost}. The plan also tracks {num_time_vars} event timestamps for key visits or handoffs. Operational policies are given as standardized rule records that link pairing choices to timing consistency requirements and to shared-facility limits aggregated over a single referenced order set (the set may cover only a subset of orders). In this instance, each order has a designated primary chassis option that shares the same identifier as the order, and timing-shift rules describe how the required time gap changes when switching from that primary option to an alternative. Each rule table uses a uniform comparison direction code, given by {pair_time_link_default_sense} for timing-shift rules and {chassis_time_default_sense} for group-aggregation rules. Timing-shift rules also rely on a shared large adjustment constant {pair_time_link_big_m}. All identifiers in the data files are zero-based. The goal is to find a feasible plan with minimum total cost.

Imagine a logistics distribution company located in the city center. The company plans to go public in 2028. In a specific mission, the company needs to rationally arrange routes among N1 possible paths to transport goods for N2 customers. Each shipment of these goods weighs W, and each delivery generates a revenue of R for the company. It is required that every customer receives a delivery exactly once. The transportation cost for each route is stored in table C1, and the list of customers reachable by each route is stored in table C2. Output the minimum cost.

Parameters (JSON): {"N1": 4516, "N2": 32, "R": 2, "W": 30}
Associated data files:
- files_1.path: data/C1.csv
- files_1.description: In Table C1, the first column contains route IDs and the second column shows the transportation cost of each corresponding route.
- files_2.path: data/C2.csv
- files_2.description: In Table C2, the first column of each row is a route ID, followed by the IDs of the customers that the route can reach.

In the strategic framework of the Regional Industrial Grid Synchronization Initiative, the primary objective is to align the discrete power injection capabilities of the Alpha-Series Modular Generators with the aggregated demand profiles of downstream heavy machinery. The Alpha-Series unit serves as the sole primary source, possessing a fixed nominal power rating of {val_a} kilowatts. The downstream consumption matrix consists of two distinct equipment classes: the Beta-Class Hydraulic Press, which draws a fixed load of {val_b} kilowatts, and the Gamma-Class Thermal Inductor, requiring {val_c} kilowatts. Operational protocols classify Beta-Class units as Sector-Red assets requiring high-frequency voltage monitoring, whereas Gamma-Class units are Sector-Blue assets monitored on standard cycles. Achieving the operational state defined as Static Load Equilibrium requires that the total power supplied by a discrete number of Alpha-Series generators equates strictly to the sum of power consumed by a combination of Beta-Class and Gamma-Class units. The electrical bus architecture dictates that all units, both source and load, must be engaged as complete, indivisible integers. Furthermore, the control logic prioritizes system stability by initiating Sector-Red units with a phase lead of 15 degrees relative to Sector-Blue units. To minimize the footprint of the primary generation bank and reduce maintenance overhead on the source side, engineers must identify the lowest possible count of Alpha-Series Generators (greater than zero) necessary to satisfy this strict equality against a valid non-negative integer configuration of load units. Additionally, the system anticipates a reactive power factor of 0.85 which is managed by separate capacitor banks.

Parameters (JSON): {"val_a": 31013, "val_b": 41014, "val_c": 51015}

EnLight is a finite combinatorial puzzle played on a rectangular board of {num_rows} rows and {num_cols} columns (with {num_cells} cells in total). Each cell of the board carries a light that can be either ON or OFF, and a move consists of selecting one cell and toggling its light together with the lights of its immediate neighbors above, below, left, and right (when those neighbors exist), creating a plus-shaped effect. The puzzle starts from a given initial position on this board, where a subset of cells is initially lit and the others are dark; this configuration is fully specified in the grid data. Because toggling the same cell twice would undo its effect, each cell is either chosen once as a move or not chosen at all. The goal is to find a sequence of moves (a subset of cells to toggle) that transforms the initial position into a terminal position where all lights are OFF, while minimizing the total number of moves played. The mixed-integer programming model for this instance represents each potential move as a binary decision variable and enforces, for every cell, that the parity of all moves affecting it is consistent with its initial ON/OFF state.

Parameters (JSON): {"num_cells": "64", "num_cols": "8", "num_moves": "64", "num_rows": "8"}
Associated data files:
- grid.path: data/grid.csv
- grid.description: Board layout and initial light configuration for all cells in the EnLight puzzle. Each row corresponds to one cell of the {num_rows} × {num_cols} board.

Columns:
- `cell_id`: 1-based identifier of the cell, running row by row from top-left to bottom-right; in this instance `cell_id = (row - 1) * {num_cols} + col`.
- `row`: row index of the cell, from 1 to {num_rows}, with 1 being the top row of the board.
- `col`: column index of the cell, from 1 to {num_cols}, with 1 being the leftmost column.
- `initial_state`: initial ON/OFF state of the light on this cell (1 = ON, 0 = OFF). The optimization model enforces that, after applying all selected moves, every cell’s final state is OFF.

In `enlight8`, the initial configuration is a specific pattern on the 8×8 board with a few lit cells; see the `initial_state` column for the exact layout.

Data format:
| cell_id | row | col | initial_state |
|---------|-----|-----|---------------|
| 1       | 1   | 1   | 1             |
| 2       | 1   | 2   | 0             |
| 3       | 1   | 3   | 0             |
| 4       | 1   | 4   | 0             |
| ...     | ... | ... | ...           |
| 64      | 8   | 8   | 0             |
- move_effects.path: data/move_effects.csv
- move_effects.description: Neighborhood effect of each move on the EnLight board. Each row states that a given move (played at some cell) affects the light state of a given target cell. In `enlight8`, every move affects its own cell and its orthogonal neighbors (up/down/left/right) when those neighbors exist, creating a plus-shaped pattern centered at the move’s cell.

Columns:
- `move_id`: identifier of the move, matching `moves.csv`.
- `move_row`: row index of the move’s center cell, repeated for readability (can be derived from `move_id`).
- `move_col`: column index of the move’s center cell, repeated for readability.
- `cell_id`: identifier of the affected cell, matching `grid.csv`.
- `cell_row`: row index of the affected cell, repeated for readability.
- `cell_col`: column index of the affected cell, repeated for readability.

In the mixed-integer model, for each cell you sum the binary move variables of all moves that affect it (as listed here), add its `initial_state`, and impose an even-parity condition so that the final light state is OFF.

Data format:
| move_id | move_row | move_col | cell_id | cell_row | cell_col |
|---------|----------|----------|---------|----------|----------|
| 1       | 1        | 1        | 1       | 1        | 1        |
| 2       | 1        | 2        | 1       | 1        | 1        |
| 9       | 2        | 1        | 1       | 1        | 1        |
| ...     | ...      | ...      | ...     | ...      | ...      |
- moves.path: data/moves.csv
- moves.description: Set of available moves in the EnLight puzzle, one potential move per board cell. Each move corresponds to choosing a cell once and applying the plus-shaped light toggle centered at that cell.

Columns:
- `move_id`: 1-based identifier of the move; in this instance, there is exactly one move per cell, so `move_id = (move_row - 1) * {num_cols} + move_col` and `num_moves = {num_moves}`.
- `move_row`: row index of the cell where the move is played (the move’s center), from 1 to {num_rows}.
- `move_col`: column index of the cell where the move is played, from 1 to {num_cols}.

In the optimization model, each move is associated with a binary decision variable indicating whether that move is used (1) or not used (0).

Data format:
| move_id | move_row | move_col |
|---------|----------|----------|
| 1       | 1        | 1        |
| 2       | 1        | 2        |
| 3       | 1        | 3        |
| 4       | 1        | 4        |
| ...     | ...      | ...      |
| 64      | 8        | 8        |

- `move_row`: row index of the cell where the move is played (the move’s center), from 1 to {num_rows}.
- `move_col`: column index of the cell where the move is played, from 1 to {num_cols}.

In the optimization model, each move is associated with a binary decision variable indicating whether that move is used (1) or not used (0).

Data format:
| move_id | move_row | move_col |
|---------|----------|----------|
| 1       | 1        | 1        |
| 2       | 1        | 2        |
| 3       | 1        | 3        |
| 4       | 1        | 4        |
| ...     | ...      | ...      |
| 100     | 10       | 10       |

This is a {n}*{n} puzzle reconstruction problem. Each puzzle piece has 4 edges: top, right, bottom, and left. Each edge is either a boundary edge marked as 0 (imagine the straight edge of a real puzzle piece) or an internal edge marked with a specific positive integer. Pieces can be rotated. (This differs slightly from the interlocking tabs and blanks of real puzzles.) The puzzle rule requires that the touching edges of adjacent puzzle pieces must have the same integer mark. Based on geometric features, the pieces are classified into three categories: 4 corner pieces have two adjacent boundary edges; 4*{n}-8 edge (non-corner) pieces have one boundary edge; and the remaining ({n}-2)^2 internal pieces have no boundary edges. The file `numbers` provides the numbers marked on the four edges of all puzzle pieces. Please find a valid puzzle layout that satisfies the rules. The objective function is the number of successfully placed puzzle pieces.

Parameters (JSON): {"n": 10}
Associated data files:
- numbers.path: ./data/numbers.csv
- numbers.description: A table containing the edge values for each puzzle piece. The columns are `piece_id`, `top_edge`, `right_edge`, `bottom_edge`, and `left_edge`. `0` represents a boundary edge, while positive integers represent internal edge marks that must match with adjacent pieces.
Data format:
| piece_id | top_edge | right_edge | bottom_edge | left_edge |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 0 | 5 | 14 | 14 | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 11 | 7 | 2 |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

We are engineering the layout for a {n}*{n} wafer-scale processor array. The system comprises square micro-processing modules, each featuring four connection ports labeled Top, Right, Bottom, and Left. The file `numbers` specifies the signal protocol ID for each port of every module. A protocol ID of 0 signifies a thermal exhaust port. To ensure proper heat dissipation, these exhaust ports must be positioned facing the external perimeter of the array chassis. Positive integer IDs represent high-speed data bus channels; for a valid connection, the touching ports of any two adjacent modules must share the identical bus channel ID. The modules are manufactured on a rigid substrate and cannot be flipped over, yet the assembly process requires determining the correct orientation for each module within its assigned slot. Based on the thermal design power (TDP) constraints, the inventory strictly contains 4 corner modules (two exhaust ports), 4*{n}-8 perimeter modules (one exhaust port), and ({n}-2)^2 core modules (no exhaust ports). Modules with Prime Number IDs are fabricated using a 7nm process, while composite IDs use a 14nm process; 7nm modules reduce the localized voltage requirement by 0.05V. The total power draw is calculated as the sum of all active bus channel IDs multiplied by the voltage coefficient. Find a valid array configuration. The objective is to maximize the number of modules successfully integrated into the chassis complying with all port compatibility and boundary thermal rules.

You are the Director of Operations for a specialized aerospace metallurgy facility responsible for casting {num_products} distinct high-entropy alloys over a fiscal planning horizon of {num_periods} discrete operational intervals. The facility utilizes a single, high-frequency induction furnace for all casting tasks. Due to strict chemical purity requirements and the risk of cross-contamination, the furnace is restricted to processing a single alloy type within any given interval. The thermodynamic properties of each alloy impose specific physical throughput limits, defined as {capacities} units per interval respectively. Initiating a production run for any alloy requires a specific thermal calibration and mold reconfiguration, incurring a one-time setup cost for that interval. Direct manufacturing expenses are recorded on a per-unit basis. Completed units not immediately shipped to the client are transferred to a secure, climate-controlled vault, accruing inventory holding costs per unit for each interval they remain in storage. Conversely, if the confirmed demand is not met within the current interval, the order is classified as a backlog. Per our Service Level Agreements, a penalty cost is charged for each backlogged unit for every interval it remains unfulfilled. Note that alloys with odd-numbered identifiers are designated for deep-space missions and require a secondary certification by an external agency after leaving our facility; this external process does not impact our internal scheduling flow. Additionally, the facility monitors 'Sustainability Credits', where each setup event consumes 5 credits from the annual allowance, tracked by the environmental department. The furnace liner degrades at a rate of 0.01% per production hour, but replacement costs are capitalized annually and excluded from this operational budget. By the conclusion of the final planning interval, all production accounts must be balanced, meaning no inventory remains in the vault and no backlogs are outstanding. Your objective is to determine the optimal production sequence that minimizes the total operational expenditure (comprising production, setup, inventory holding, and backlog penalty costs) based on the rates provided in the accounting files.

The coverage threshold required for each risk exposure is provided in Table C3. Under the Strategic Logistics Modernization Roadmap, a trans-continental railway operator is finalizing the crew roster for the upcoming fiscal quarter. The operations research division has pre-computed {n_packages} compliant crew pairings (referred to as 'packages') that strictly adhere to union regulations regarding maximum duty hours and mandatory rest periods. These packages are designed to service a network comprising {n_tasks} essential transport legs (tasks). The explicit mapping between these potential packages and the specific tasks they service is defined in the `package_task` dataset. Financial constraints dictate a rigorous cost minimization strategy for the current scheduling phase. Packages are categorized into two tiers as listed in `package_difficulty`: 'easy' and 'hard'. An 'easy' package represents a standard day shift with a normalized operational cost of 1 unit. A 'hard' package involves complex inter-regional transfers and overnight lodging, resulting in a cost structure that is exactly double that of a standard 'easy' shift. Additionally, 'hard' packages are flagged for the company's Carbon Net-Zero dashboard as they contribute an estimated 4.5 tons of CO2 equivalent per instance, whereas 'easy' packages contribute 1.2 tons, affecting the annual sustainability rating. Upon final selection of the schedule, the legal department will conduct a compliance audit on the chosen roster, charging an administrative processing fee equal to 3% of the total schedule cost, which is payable in the subsequent financial year. The immediate objective is to determine the subset of packages that covers every single task at least once to ensure network continuity, minimizing the total direct operational cost (sum of package costs) for the current quarter.

A commercial aviation enterprise is charting its fleet operations strategy for the upcoming semi-annual cycle, facing a dynamic market where travel demand fluctuates month by month. The central challenge lies in orchestrating a fleet composition that remains responsive to these shifting needs without incurring excessive financial liabilities. Strategic planners must navigate a trade-off between leveraging their existing, high-capacity standard fleet and integrating temporary rental units that offer flexibility but come with different operational profiles.

The operational landscape is governed by strict resource constraints: while the core fleet provides a reliable baseline of flight hours, rental additions are available to bridge capacity gaps but require integration time, effectively offering fewer service hours upon induction. Furthermore, the workforce has limits-overtime can be utilized to squeeze more flight hours out of the existing lineup, but this comes at a premium and is capped to prevent burnout. Complicating matters is the natural attrition of the fleet and specific regulatory bounds on fleet size that kick in after the initial month. The objective is to devise a master schedule that dictates aircraft acquisition, retention, and utilization levels for each month, ensuring every hour of projected passenger demand is met. Success is measured by the ability to deliver this service continuity at the absolute lowest total cost, factoring in the expenses of operating standard units, commissioning rentals, and paying for overtime labor.

Assume you are the logistics director of a polar expedition. You need to transport N different types of modular equipment to a remote base. Each equipment type has a specific 'Scientific Value' (or Cost), as shown in Table C1. The transportation mission is subject to M1 types of resource constraints (such as weight, volume, etc.) and must meet M2 types of performance requirements (such as power generation, thermal protection, etc.). 

The resource consumption of each equipment type is listed in Table C2, and the maximum limit for each resource is listed in Table C3. The performance contribution of each equipment type to the required metrics is listed in Table C4, and the minimum required standard for each performance metric is listed in Table C5.

Please output the quantity of each equipment type to be transported to minimize the total cost (or maximize value) while satisfying all resource limits and meeting all performance standards.

Parameters (JSON): {"M1": 4, "M2": 20, "N": 28}
Associated data files:
- files_1.path: data/C1.csv
- files_1.description: Table C1: The first column is the Equipment Type ID. The second column represents the coefficient in the objective function (Scientific Value or Cost) for each equipment type.
- files_2.path: data/C2.csv
- files_2.description: Table C2: Resource Consumption Matrix. The rows represent Equipment Type IDs, and the columns represent the M1 Resource Constraint IDs . The value at each intersection represents the consumption of a specific resource by a specific equipment type.
- files_3.path: data/C3.csv
- files_3.description: Table C3: Resource Limits. The first column lists the Resource Constraint IDs , and the second column shows the maximum allowable limit (Upper Bound) for each resource.
- files_4.path: data/C4.csv
- files_4.description: Table C4: Performance Contribution Matrix. The rows represent Equipment Type IDs, and the columns represent the M2 Performance Requirement IDs . The value at each intersection represents the contribution of a specific equipment type to a specific performance metric.
- files_5.path: data/C5.csv
- files_5.description: Table C5: Performance Targets. The first column lists the Performance Requirement IDs , and the second column shows the minimum required value (Lower Bound) for each performance metric.

We need to develop an optimal harvest schedule for a large-scale forestry region. The region is divided into M distinct timber stands (harvest units), and the planning horizon spans T operational periods (batches indexed 0 to T−1). Our primary objective is to maximize total revenue by making decisions based on the estimated timber value of each stand in each period (data provided in R.csv).

In this plan, the status of each stand in each period is binary: either "harvest" or "reserve." To ensure operational sustainability and regulatory compliance, the following three business rules must be strictly observed:

1. Operational Conflict Rules (Mutually Exclusive Activities) Due to constraints such as geographical proximity, road usage, or equipment scheduling, conflicts exist between certain harvest plans. Table C1.csv lists several groups of "mutually exclusive activity lists" (one group per row). For the "Stand-Period" combinations listed in each group, at most one may be approved for execution to avoid operational conflicts.

2. Supply Chain Stability (Harvest Volume Smoothing) To ensure a stable supply of raw materials for downstream processing plants, we cannot harvest excessively in one year and have no supply the next. Therefore, the total harvest volume in each period must transition smoothly relative to the previous period. Specifically, for batches 2 through (T−1), the total harvest volume of each batch must be no less than N1 times that of the preceding batch and no greater than N2 times that of the preceding batch. C2.csv provides the estimated yield for each stand in different periods.

3. Ecological Red Line Mechanism (Environmental Loss-Gain Balance) We must balance economic interests with ecological protection. Certain harvesting activities cause environmental damage (negative values indicate loss), while other specific operations (such as thinning or clearing diseased trees) may bring ecological improvements (positive values indicate gain). Table C3.csv defines multiple ecological assessment metrics. For each metric, the positive ecological gains generated by our selected plan must be sufficient to offset the negative environmental losses (i.e., the net impact value must be non-negative), thereby ensuring the balance of the ecosystem.

Parameters (JSON): {"M": 319, "N1": 0.8, "N2": 1.2, "T": 6}
Associated data files:
- files_1.path: data/C1.csv
- files_1.description: Each row represents a group of mutually exclusive harvest activities, from which only one can be selected. An activity involving the harvesting of unit i in batch j is denoted in the table by the tuple (i,j).
- files_2.path: data/C2.csv
- files_2.description: C2: The first column lists the harvest units, and the first row lists the harvest batches. The value located at a specific row and column represents the harvest volume obtained from harvesting the corresponding unit in the corresponding batch.
- files_3.path: data/C3.csv
- files_3.description: C3: Each row represents a constraint and contains several triples (y,i,j).

If y<0, it indicates that harvesting unit i in batch j results in an environmental loss of y.

If y>0, it indicates that harvesting unit i in batch j generates an environmental gain of y.
- files_4.path: data/R.csv
- files_4.description: R.csv: The first column lists the harvest units, and the first row lists the harvest batches. The value at a specific row and column represents the final revenue obtained from harvesting the corresponding unit in the corresponding batch. Our objective is to maximize this revenue.

We need to develop an optimal capital investment schedule for a global corporation. The corporation has identified M potential investment projects (units). The planning horizon spans T financial quarters (batches indexed 0 to T-1). Our primary objective is to maximize total Return on Investment (ROI) by deciding which projects to fund in which quarter based on estimated returns (data provided in R.csv).

In this plan, the status of each project in each quarter is binary: either "fund" or "hold." To ensure financial stability and strategic alignment, the following three business rules must be observed:

1. Strategic Conflict Rules (Mutually Exclusive Investments): Due to market overlap, exclusive partnerships, or resource competition, certain projects cannot be funded simultaneously. Table C1.csv lists groups of "mutually exclusive investment opportunities." For the "Project-Quarter" combinations in each group, at most one can be selected.

2. Cash Flow Stability (Capital Deployment Smoothing): To ensure steady liquidity and avoid shocking the balance sheet, capital deployment cannot fluctuate wildly. The total capital deployed in each quarter must transition smoothly. Specifically, for quarters 2 through (T-1), total investment volume must be no less than N1 times and no greater than N2 times that of the preceding quarter. C2.csv provides the required capital for each project.

3. ESG Compliance Mechanism (Risk-Impact Balance): We must balance high-yield/high-risk projects with sustainable initiatives. Some projects have negative ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) scores (losses), while others provide positive credits (gains). Table C3.csv defines ESG metrics. For each metric, the positive credits from selected projects must offset the negative impacts, ensuring a non-negative net ESG score.

Parameters (JSON): {"M": 319, "N1": 0.8, "N2": 1.2, "T": 6}
Associated data files:
- files_1.path: data/C1.csv
- files_1.description: Each row represents a group of mutually exclusive investment options. An activity involving funding project i in quarter j is denoted by (i,j).
- files_2.path: data/C2.csv
- files_2.description: C2: The value located at a specific row and column represents the capital requirement for funding the corresponding project in the corresponding quarter.
- files_3.path: data/C3.csv
- files_3.description: C3: Each row represents an ESG constraint containing triples (y,i,j). If y<0, funding project i in quarter j causes an ESG deficit of y. If y>0, it generates an ESG credit of y.
- files_4.path: data/R.csv
- files_4.description: R.csv: The value at a specific row and column represents the projected ROI/Profit from funding the corresponding project in the corresponding quarter.

We are playing a script-based murder mystery game where I play the owner of a freight company with a total market value of W billion. I have been assigned a task: transport goods from N1 factories to N2 customers. We must select specific routes to open from N1*N2 possible options and determine the quantity of goods transported on each route (which does not have to be an integer). When selecting routes, I must communicate with at least N game DMs. Opening each route requires paying a fixed cost, as shown in Table C1. Additionally, transporting one unit of cargo on each route incurs a shipping fee, as shown in Table C2. The quantity of goods produced by each factory is fixed, as shown in Table C3; the quantity transported out of a factory must equal its production volume. The quantity of goods required by each customer is also fixed, as shown in Table C4; the quantity received by each customer must equal their demand. Our objective is to determine the minimum cost to be paid while satisfying all conditions.

Associated data files:

- files_2.path: data/C2.csv
- files_2.description: The first column of Table C₂ lists factory numbers, and the first row lists customer numbers. The value at each row-column intersection represents the unit shipping cost for transporting goods via the route from the corresponding factory to the corresponding customer.
- files_3.path: data/C3.csv
- files_3.description: The first column of Table C₃ lists factory numbers, and the second column lists the production volume of each factory—all goods produced must be shipped out in full.
- files_4.path: data/C4.csv
- files_4.description: The first column of Table C₄ lists customer numbers, and the second column lists the demand volume of each customer—the quantity of goods received by the customer must exactly meet its demand.

The Emerald Valley ecosystem management project involves {n} observation outposts connected by {m} logistical trails defined in the network topology. To mitigate the risk of pathogen propagation, the administration intends to establish multiple layers of Ecological Isolation Barriers. Functionally, establishing one barrier layer necessitates a global binary partition of the entire set of outposts into two distinct, non-communicating operational zones. Once a partition is defined, every trail connecting an outpost in one zone to an outpost in the other is fitted with a specialized bio-scanner. Outposts with even indices are designated as High-Priority Data Hubs, responsible for aggregating sensor metrics, whereas odd-indexed outposts serve as transmission relays for the collected data. Due to the physical narrowness of the trails, a specific trail can accommodate hardware for at most one barrier layer. Additionally, the power grid supplying the trails provides 220V AC with a 15A limit, and each bio-scanner draws approximately 14.5A when active to ensure full sterilization efficacy. The project financing is structured such that the first three barriers deployed will receive a federal subsidy of 15 percent, amortized over a decade. The primary objective is to determine the upper bound on the count of distinct barrier layers that can be simultaneously active within the network topology, ensuring no single trail is overloaded with hardware from multiple layers.

Within the framework of the Urban Resilience Project involving {n} logistic hubs and {m} transit corridors, the administration intends to implement a series of parallel 'Bio-Safety Cordon Layers'. A valid Cordon Layer is operationally defined as a structural partition that segregates the entire set of hubs into two disjoint functional zones, necessitating strict sanitation checkpoints on all corridors bridging these two zones. Due to voltage regulation constraints on the corridor infrastructure, the auxiliary power supply of any single corridor can energize at most one sanitation checkpoint system. Consequently, a corridor utilized by one Cordon Layer becomes unavailable for any others. Additionally, hubs with identifiers divisible by three are categorized as Cold-Chain Primary Nodes, featuring reinforced geological foundations rated Class A, suitable for heavy equipment. The fiscal projection model indicates that the annual sustainment cost for each deployed Cordon Layer equates to 12 percent of the initial installation capital, subject to quarterly audits. The optimization task is to compute the supreme cardinality of such Cordon Layers that can be simultaneously established on the existing topology without any corridor overlap.

A critical infrastructure protection project involves a network of {n} distinct processing nodes and {m} physical transmission conduits. The system architecture requires the implementation of multiple parallel 'Cyber-Physical Segmentation Layers' (CPSLs) to achieve depth-in-defense. Each CPSL is defined as a topological partition that strictly separates the entire set of nodes into two disjoint operational domains (Domain Alpha and Domain Beta), necessitating the deployment of hardware-based traffic inspection probes on every conduit bridging these two domains. Nodes are categorized into Primary and Secondary tiers based on their processing throughput, where Primary nodes account for approximately 35% of the total energy consumption. Physical constraints on the conduit interface ports dictate that each transmission conduit can accommodate the hardware for exactly one inspection probe, meaning a conduit assigned to one CPSL cannot be utilized by another. The deployment of each probe incurs a signal latency penalty of 0.4ms and a capital expenditure of 1,500 units. The objective is to determine the theoretical upper limit of the number of independent CPSLs that can be simultaneously active within the network without any conduit resource conflicts.

To analyze deep crustal stress accumulation, the system needs to configure multiple parallel instances of 'Tectonic Stress Isolation Protocols'. Structurally, a single protocol instance requires the entire network of outposts to be partitioned into exactly two disconnected geological zones (Zone Alpha and Zone Beta), where the cables connecting these two zones are repurposed to transmit high-frequency interference signals. Several operational parameters are defined: First, outposts are classified based on their numerical ID: those with odd IDs are designated as 'Master Nodes' which process data throughput at 1.5 times the rate of the 'Slave Nodes' (even IDs) to facilitate regional aggregation. Second, due to the physical properties of the optical fiber cores, a cable utilized for interference signal transmission in one protocol becomes frequency-locked and cannot be reused by any other protocol instance. Third, the project's long-term sustainability assessment relies on annual maintenance fees, calculated as 12% of the initial installation cost for each active cable, while the initial installation costs are considered sunk. The objective is to determine the maximum theoretical number of independent Tectonic Stress Isolation Protocols that can be simultaneously active on the existing network topology, such that no two protocols share the same cable resource.

We are optimizing the thermal isolation architecture for a massive subterranean data center comprising {n} server clusters and {m} coolant circulation pipes. The server clusters are categorized by their processing architecture: clusters with odd identifiers are GPU-heavy nodes, while those with even identifiers are CPU-centric nodes. To mitigate the risk of catastrophic overheating spreading across the facility, the safety protocol requires the installation of multiple independent 'Thermal Firewalls'. A Thermal Firewall is defined as a system state that completely segregates the entire facility into two distinct, non-communicating thermal zones (Zone Hot and Zone Cold) by shutting off all coolant flow between them. Due to the limited physical clearance within the coolant pipes, each pipe can accommodate the mechanical valve assembly for exactly one Thermal Firewall. This implies that no two Firewalls can utilize the same pipe for interception. Firewalls are classified into 'Active' (utilizing powered pumps) and 'Passive' (utilizing gravity blocks) types based on the flow direction, yet both contribute equally to the total safety score. The installation of each Firewall incurs a base cost of 50,000 credits plus a variable maintenance fee based on the depth of the pipe. With an unlimited budget and inventory for valve assemblies, calculate the maximum possible number of distinct Thermal Firewalls that can be simultaneously deployed in the network.

A team maintains a grid-based architecture diagram with {N} entities and {E} relationships between some entity pairs. Each entity must be placed at an integer (row, column) location within its own allowed window. The layout should remain readable: no two entities may overlap, directly related entities should stay close to reduce visual clutter, and the diagram should not drift too far from a given anchor position for each entity.

For every unordered pair of entities, the layout must choose exactly one of four non-overlap options: separate them on the row axis (with one order), separate them on the row axis (with the opposite order), separate them on the column axis (with one order), or separate them on the column axis (with the opposite order). Each option has its own required minimum separation, provided in the data. To avoid contradictory “before/after” decisions along the same axis, these choices must be globally consistent across all triples of entities on each axis.

For each relationship edge, the model measures row and column separations with auxiliary nonnegative distance values linked to the endpoint coordinates by linear inequalities. The objective minimizes {edge_weight} times the total edge separation plus {center_weight} times the total deviation from anchors. The instance also enforces a required minimum total anchor deviation of {center_sum_lb}.

All CSV files live in the `data/` folder next to this JSON. Use numeric IDs for joins; labels are for readability only.

Parameters (JSON): {"E": "11", "N": "8", "center_sum_lb": "22", "center_weight": "1", "edge_weight": "64", "pair_count": "28"}
Associated data files:
- edge_distance_params.path: data/edge_distance_params.csv
- edge_distance_params.description: Axis-specific linearization data for each edge. There are two rows per edge, one for the row axis and one for the column axis. These rows provide constants used to link an edge’s nonnegative axis-separation value to the difference of endpoint coordinates on that axis.

Reconstruction notes:
- For each edge and each axis, create a nonnegative axis-separation value `d_axis` and add the two inequalities:
  - `d_axis + (pos_axis[v] - pos_axis[u]) >= rhs_for_v_minus_u`
  - `d_axis + (pos_axis[u] - pos_axis[v]) >= rhs_for_u_minus_v`
  where `u` and `v` are the edge endpoints from `edges.csv`.
- Also enforce the total (row+col) separation lower bound using the edge’s `manhattan_lb` from `edges.csv`.
- Use the chosen placement option for the same unordered endpoint pair in `pairs.csv` to determine whether the pair is separated on the row axis or on the column axis:
  - Let `z_row` be the sum of the two row-separation options for that pair.
  - Let `z_col` be the sum of the two column-separation options for that pair.
  Then apply:
  - If `axis = row`: `d_row + min_axis_distance_if_axis_chosen * z_col >= min_axis_distance_if_axis_chosen`
  - If `axis = col`: `d_col + min_axis_distance_if_axis_chosen * z_row >= min_axis_distance_if_axis_chosen`

Data format:
| edge_id | axis | rhs_for_v_minus_u | rhs_for_u_minus_v | min_axis_distance_if_axis_chosen | distance_ub |
|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | row | ... | ... | ... | ... |

- edges.path: data/edges.csv
- edges.description: Relationship edges. Each row identifies a connected entity pair whose separation contributes to the objective.

Notes:
- Endpoints are numeric IDs referencing `entities.csv`.
- Do not join by label.

Data format:
| edge_id | u_id | v_id | weight | manhattan_lb |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | ... | ... | ... | ... |

- entities.path: data/entities.csv
- entities.description: Entity list. Each row describes one diagram box, including its allowed placement window on the discrete grid and its anchor (reference) position.

Notes:
- Use `entity_id` as the primary key for joins across files.
- The `label` field is only for human readability; do not join by label.

Data format:
| entity_id | label | row_min | row_max | col_min | col_max | anchor_row | anchor_col |
|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | EntityA | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

- pairs.path: data/pairs.csv
- pairs.description: Unordered entity pairs and their non-overlap requirements. Each row represents one unordered pair, stored once using a fixed ordering. For each pair, the layout must choose exactly one of four placement options.

Columns (conceptual meaning):
- `row_gap_i_before_j`: the minimum row separation if the pair is separated on the row axis and `i_id` is before `j_id`.
- `row_gap_j_before_i`: the minimum row separation if the pair is separated on the row axis and `j_id` is before `i_id`.
- `col_gap_i_before_j`: the minimum column separation if the pair is separated on the column axis and `i_id` is before `j_id`.
- `col_gap_j_before_i`: the minimum column separation if the pair is separated on the column axis and `j_id` is before `i_id`.

Reconstruction notes:
- For each `pair_id`, create four yes/no choice variables representing the four options above and enforce they sum to 1.
- If a row-separation option is chosen, enforce the corresponding row difference (based on direction) to be at least the matching `row_gap_*` value. If a column-separation option is chosen, enforce the corresponding column difference to be at least the matching `col_gap_*` value.
- Define directed “before/after on an axis” indicators for any ordered pair of entities `(a,b)` as:
  - `row_before[a,b]` is the chosen row-separation option that places `a` before `b` (look it up from the unique unordered pair row in this file).
  - `col_before[a,b]` is the chosen column-separation option that places `a` before `b`.
- For every triple of distinct entities `(a,b,c)` and for each axis separately, prevent 3-cycles using:
  - `before[a,b] + before[b,c] + before[c,a] <= 2`
  - `before[a,c] + before[c,b] + before[b,a] <= 2`
  Apply these twice: once with `before = row_before`, and once with `before = col_before`.

Data format:
| pair_id | i_id | j_id | row_gap_i_before_j | row_gap_j_before_i | col_gap_i_before_j | col_gap_j_before_i |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

A team maintains a grid-based architecture diagram with {N} entities and {E} relationships between some entity pairs. Each entity must be placed at an integer (row, column) location within its own allowed window. The layout should remain readable: no two entities may overlap, directly related entities should stay close to reduce visual clutter, and the diagram should not drift too far from a given anchor position for each entity.

For every unordered pair of entities, the layout must choose exactly one of four non-overlap options: separate them on the row axis (with one order), separate them on the row axis (with the opposite order), separate them on the column axis (with one order), or separate them on the column axis (with the opposite order). Each option has its own required minimum separation, provided in the data. To avoid contradictory “before/after” decisions along the same axis, these choices must be globally consistent across all triples of entities on each axis.

For each relationship edge, the model measures row and column separations with auxiliary nonnegative distance values linked to the endpoint coordinates by linear inequalities. The objective minimizes a weighted sum of total edge separation (edge weights are given in the data) plus {center_weight} times the total deviation from anchors. The instance also enforces a required minimum total anchor deviation of {center_sum_lb}.

We are deploying a High-Precision Stratified Diagnostic System for a clinical trial involving {n_samples} participants, each characterized by {n_features} transcriptomic markers. The primary objective is to construct a linear decision boundary (characterized by coefficient vector u and intercept b) that enforces sparsity to identify key biomarkers. Participants are indexed sequentially; those with IDs 1 through 250 constitute the derivation cohort, while the remainder form the validation cohort, though the optimization is performed jointly on the full dataset. The system operates under a 'Hybrid-Triage' protocol. For each participant, the model determines whether to issue an automated diagnosis or to flag the case for Manual Expert Review. Automated diagnoses are subject to a strict safety margin; any violation of this margin results in a penalty proportional to the magnitude of the violation (Hinge Loss). Alternatively, the model may opt to flag a case (setting a binary indicator z=1), which incurs a fixed 'Administrative Cost' rather than the standard error penalty. This Administrative Cost is strictly calculated as {cost_ratio_outlier} times the specific class weight of the participant. The class weights are defined as {C_global} divided by the class count ({n_pos} for positive, {n_neg} for negative) to ensure risk parity. Crucially, the flagging mechanism provides a relaxation of the safety margin by a magnitude of {M} (the Capacity Parameter). Should the margin violation exceed {M}, the surplus error is deemed a 'Critical Failure' and is added to the loss function even for flagged cases. The optimization goal is to minimize the total structural risk, defined as the sum of the L1 norm of u (for feature selection), the cumulative penalties for automated diagnosis errors, and the total Administrative Costs for flagged cases. Note that selected biomarkers with non-zero coefficients will undergo wet-lab PCR validation costing 150 currency units per marker in a subsequent phase, and the Manual Expert Review board has a maximum throughput of 50 cases per week, necessitating a strategic balance in the modeling phase.

We are engineering a high-dimensional linear discriminator for a precision oncology screening program involving {n_samples} clinical subjects. The dataset includes {n_features} gene expression markers per subject. The cohort is divided into {n_pos} confirmed pathology cases and {n_neg} control subjects. Due to potential thermal fluctuations during the PCR amplification cycles, particularly in batches processed during peak hours, the modeling framework must incorporate a noise-rejection mechanism. This mechanism allows the solver to categorize specific subjects as 'conforming' or 'deviant' via a binary decision variable. The optimization goal is to minimize a composite cost function consisting of three components. First, to enforce model sparsity and identify biomarker signatures, the L1 norm of the feature weight vector u is minimized. Second, for subjects categorized as 'conforming', the model incurs a weighted Hinge Loss relative to a standard classification margin of 1. Third, subjects flagged as 'deviant' bypass the standard margin requirement but incur a fixed 'manual review' penalty cost. This fixed penalty is calculated as {cost_ratio_outlier} times the class-specific importance weight of the subject. The importance weights are derived by distributing a global regularization intensity {C_global} inversely proportional to the number of samples in each class to ensure actuarial fairness. A saturation parameter {M} is introduced to mathematically relax the constraints for deviant samples. It is important to note that due to the finite nature of this saturation limit, any margin violation that geographically exceeds {M} will still contribute residual penalties to the objective function, even if the sample is flagged as deviant. The top 5% of samples with the highest decision values will be prioritized for longitudinal follow-up studies next year. The solver must determine the optimal weights u and bias b.

In the context of a high-throughput bio-security screening system deployment, we are tasked with configuring a linear decision boundary (defined by feature weight vector u and offset b) to differentiate between pathogenic and non-pathogenic signatures based on {n_features} distinct spectral markers obtained from {n_samples} vials. The spectral sensors 1 through 5 operate at a higher frequency of 50Hz compared to the standard 20Hz for the remaining sensors, ensuring higher temporal resolution for those specific bands. The system utilizes a 'Dynamic Routing Protocol' to manage sample integrity. Under this protocol, the algorithm must assign each sample to one of two processing streams: the 'Automated Verification Stream' or the 'Manual Audit Stream'. For samples routed to the Automated Verification Stream, strict adherence to a separation margin is required to ensure statistical significance; any violation of this margin incurs a 'Compliance Risk Penalty' proportional to the magnitude of the violation. Conversely, samples routed to the Manual Audit Stream are removed from the automated classification queue, bypassing the margin check entirely. Routing a sample to the Manual Stream incurs a fixed 'Administrative Overhead'. The Administrative Overhead for a single sample is calculated as the cost of exactly two units of that sample's Base Priority Score. The system's objective is to minimize the 'Total Operational Burden', which is the sum of three components: 1. The Sensor Sparsity Cost (measured by the L1 norm of u to minimize bandwidth); 2. The total Compliance Risk Penalties from the Automated Stream; 3. The total Administrative Overheads from the Manual Stream. The Base Priority Score is derived from a global regulation constant {C_global}, distributed to correct for class prevalence: pathogenic samples carry a score of {C_global} divided by {n_pos}, while non-pathogenic samples carry a score of {C_global} divided by {n_neg}. The facility's total power consumption is capped at 1200W.

This is a robust classification modeling problem in medical diagnosis. Given gene expression data for {n_samples} patients with {n_features} features, we aim to train a robust linear classifier (weights u, intercept b). To handle data noise, the model effectively chooses whether to treat each sample as 'normal' or 'outlier'. For normal samples, the model enforces classification margins, and any violation incurs a Hinge Loss penalty. For outliers, the model discards the classification constraint (ignoring the error) but incurs a fixed penalty cost instead. The objective is to minimize the total sum of: 1. Regularization term (L1 norm of u, for feature selection); 2. Weighted Hinge Loss for normal samples; 3. Fixed penalties for outliers. Parameters: The fixed penalty cost is 2 times the unit Hinge Loss weight of that sample. Weights are class-balanced: {C_global}/{n_pos} for positive and {C_global}/{n_neg} for negative samples.

We are tasked with optimizing a linear compliance scoring system for {n_samples} financial entities, each characterized by {n_features} quantitative risk indicators. The goal is to determine the optimal weight vector u and intercept b to minimize a tripartite operational cost function. The first component is a sparsity penalty defined by the L1 norm of u, ensuring the model relies on a minimal set of indicators. The second and third components define the classification loss: the system dynamically assigns each entity to either a 'Standard Protocol' or an 'Exception Protocol'. Entities under the Standard Protocol enforce a strict margin constraint; any violation of this margin results in a variable penalty proportional to the violation magnitude (Weighted Hinge Loss). Entities assigned to the Exception Protocol are granted a relaxation buffer of size {M}, effectively ignoring margin violations up to this limit. However, activating the Exception Protocol incurs a fixed 'Administrative Fee' for that entity, calculated as {cost_ratio_outlier} times the entity's specific importance weight. It is crucial to note that the relaxation buffer is finite; if an Exception entity's violation exceeds {M}, the residual violation beyond {M} is still charged as a variable penalty. The importance weights are stratified by class to ensure fairness: {C_global}/{n_pos} for positive class entities and {C_global}/{n_neg} for negative class entities. Regarding the input data structure, features indexed 1 through 10 are derived from high-frequency trading logs, while the remaining features come from quarterly balance sheets. The high-frequency logs are subject to a 5ms latency during real-time inference, whereas balance sheet data is static. Additionally, the IT department charges a maintenance fee of 0.01 per non-zero weight per year after deployment. The optimization problem solely concerns the minimization of the training objective function (Sparsity + Variable Penalties + Administrative Fees).

Suppose you are the Dispatch Director of a large logistics company with an annual salary of W. A company mission requires transporting goods to N1 different destinations. The company possesses N2 different types of vehicles, with heavy-duty vehicles accounting for a proportion of L. The quantity of each vehicle type is shown in Table C1; however, these are all freight trucks and cannot compare to the Rolls-Royce at your home, which is valued at M. The cost for each vehicle type to transport to different locations is shown in Table C2, and the quantity of goods each vehicle type can transport to different locations is shown in Table C3. The goods required by each destination are shown in Table C4. Please output the minimum cost to complete the requirements.

A telecommunications corporation is architecting a next-generation fiber optic backbone network to interconnect {num_nodes} regional data centers, identified by IDs 1 through {num_nodes}. The primary uplink hub is located at node {source_node}, possessing a total outbound bandwidth capacity of {total_supply} units. The remaining nodes act as downstream distribution points or transit relays. The specific net bandwidth requirements (demand) or auxiliary injection capabilities (supply) are detailed in the `nodes` file, where positive integers denote supply capacity and negative integers denote bandwidth deficits. Zero values imply the node serves purely as a passive optical splitter for transit. The network topology must strictly adhere to a loop-free architecture to prevent broadcast storms, ensuring every node has at most one incoming uplink connection. Connections between centers are defined in `edges`, characterized by a base trenching fee (fixed cost) and a photonic amplification cost per unit of data flux (variable cost). To facilitate inventory management, nodes with odd IDs are classified as 'Legacy-Alpha' clusters using air cooling, whereas even-numbered nodes are 'Quantum-Beta' clusters utilizing liquid cooling. Additionally, the projected annual maintenance expenditure for any established link is set at 8.5% of its base trenching fee, chargeable to the OPEX budget in subsequent fiscal years. The optimization goal is to determine the minimum Total Capital Expenditure (CAPEX) required for the initial setup, which comprises the sum of all chosen trenching fees and the total photonic amplification costs based on the flow allocation.

You are a logistics scheduler faced with a massive selection problem. Instead of planning routes from scratch, you have a pool of N pre-generated 'Candidate Delivery Schedules' (Variables). Each schedule is a complete plan for a vehicle, with a calculated operational cost. On the other hand, you have M specific 'Service Requirements' (Constraints), such as 'Customer X must be visited' or 'Vehicle Y can only be used once'. Your task is to select a subset of these candidate schedules to ensure that every Service Requirement is met exactly once (sum of selected schedules = 1), while minimizing the total cost of the selected schedules.

Parameters (JSON): {"M": 39, "N": 20315}
Associated data files:
- files_1.path: data/C1.csv
- files_1.description: Table C1: The first column is the Candidate Route ID (Variable Name), and the second column is the Cost of executing this route (Objective Coefficient).
- files_2.path: data/C2.csv
- files_2.description: Table C2: This table represents the coverage relationship between Routes and Requirements. It has two columns: 'Constraint_ID' and 'Covering_Route_ID'. If a row is (R1, C1), it means choosing Route C1 contributes to satisfying Requirement R1. The problem requires that for each Constraint_ID, the sum of selected Covering_Route_IDs equals 1.

The Global Isotope Logistics Authority manages a specialized supply chain network across {n} key regional hubs. These hubs are categorized based on their net inventory position: production sites possess a surplus (negative net requirement) while clinical centers possess a deficit (positive net requirement). The aggregate output across the network matches the aggregate clinical need. Connectivity is provided by {m} dedicated transit vectors. Utilization of a vector in a given direction mandates the payment of a non-recoverable route commissioning fee to local security contractors, alongside a volumetric handling charge applied to each unit of cargo transported. It is noteworthy that hubs with an identification number divisible by three are designated as High-Security Zones, subject to an independent annual radiation safety audit, the results of which are published separately from the logistics budget. Furthermore, to adhere to hazardous material transport regulations, the flow volume on any active vector cannot exceed {capacity} units. Vectors established with a commissioning fee exceeding the 90th percentile of all network fees will be nominated for the 'Infrastructure Excellence Award' at the end of the fiscal year. The organization seeks to calculate the lowest possible total outlay required to balance the inventory across the network, accounting for strictly the route commissioning fees and volumetric handling charges for the current period.
